boardgame: Sails of Glory Ship Pack: Diana 1792 / Proserpina 1797
description:
The Diana was one of the six Mahonesas-class frigates built at the Mahón shipyard in Minorca, based on French and British construction techniques and improving upon them – She was the fastest ship of her class. It participated in the evacuation of the Spanish forces at the Siege of Roses during the French Revolutionary Wars. On 13 October 1796, she was captured after an action against HMS Terpsichore at the Strait of Gibraltar, nearby Cape Gata.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the Spanish Mahonesa class frigate “Proserpina 1797”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory: Ship Pack – Mahonesa 1789 / Ninfa 1795
description:
The Santa Monica – aka Mahonesa – was the first of a series of 6 frigates of 34 guns, built in the Minorcan port of Mahón, and had as her first service the transport of troops of Mallorca. This ship performed various services along the Mediterranean sea, and took part to French Revolutionary Wars until she was captured by the HMS Terpsichore on October 13, 1796 on the Cape of Gata, after a fight of three hours with 21 dead crewmen.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the Spanish Mahonesa class frigate “Ninfa 1795”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory: Ship Pack – HMS Africa 1781 / HMS Vigilant 1774
description:
The Inflexible–class ships were designed for the Royal Navy by Sir Thomas Slade, based on his earlier design for the Albion class. These ships were armed with 26 24–Pounder guns in the gundeck, 26 18–Pounder guns in the upperdeck, 10 4–Pounder guns in the quarterdeck, and 2 9–Pounder guns in the forecastle.

HMS Africa participated to the last battle of the American War of Independence, at Cuddalore in 1783. She returned to England once news of the peace arrived and was present at the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805, engaging the combined Franco–Spanish fleet in battle. She later fought in the Baltic Sea during the Gunboat War and, under the command of Captain John Bastard, Africa was part of the War of 1812, failing to catch the USS Constitution.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the British Intrepid class “HMS Vigilant 1774”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory: Ship Pack – HMS Polyphemus 1782 / HMS America 1777
description:
The Intrepid–class ships were designed for the Royal Navy by Sir John Williams. These ships were armed with 26 24–Pounder guns in the gundeck, 26 18–Pounder guns in the upperdeck, 10 4–Pounder guns in the quarterdeck, and 2 9–Pounder guns in the forecastle.

HMS Polyphemus was laid down at Sheerness in 1782 and commissioned in 1799 under the command of Captain G. Lumsdaine. She sailed in the North Sea Fleet on the Baltic Sea until 1802. In 1805, Capt. Robert Redmill commanded her in the Battle of Trafalgar, where she engaged the French ships Neptune and Achille and captured the Spanish ship Argonauta. She was later in service along the French coast and in the Americas.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the British Intrepid class ship of the line “HMS America 1777”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory: Ship Pack – HMS Agamemnon 1781 / HMS Raisonnable 1768
description:
The Ardent class ships were designed for the Royal Navy by Sir Thomas Slade using as a reference the French ship Fougueux. These ships were armed with 26 24–Pounder guns in the lower deck, 26 18–Pounder guns in the upperdeck, 10 9–Pounder guns in the quarterdeck, 2 9–Pounder guns in the forecastle.

HMS Agamemnon sailed and fought in the American Revolutionary, French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, taking part in the major naval battles of those conflicts. Lord Nelson served as captain on this ship during her service in the Mediterranean Sea. She was frequently in need of repair and refitting, but she fought at the Battle of Trafalgar forcing the surrender of the Spanish Santísima Trinidad. This ship ended her career in South America, wrecking on the shoal of Rio de la Plata.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the British Ardent class ship of the line “HMS Raisonnable 1768”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory: Ship Pack – HMS Malta 1800 / HMS Tonnant 1798
description:
The Tonnant class ship HMS Malta previously served with the French Navy as the “Guillaume Tell”, until she was captured in the Mediterranean Sea by the British Navy during the blockade of Malta. As Guillaume Tell, she took part in the Battle of the Nile, becoming one of only two French ships to escape the destruction of the fleet, and took refuge at Malta, where was captured. After her capture, she sailed in the British Navy for 40 years, with missions in the Channel, the Atlantic and the Mediterranean.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the British Tonnant class ship of the line “HMS Tonnant 1798”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory: Ship Pack – Neptune 1803 / Ville de Varsovie 1808
description:
The Bucentaure class ship Neptune was launched at the beginning of the Napoleonic Wars. After missions on the West Indies, she was used against the British in Mediterranean Sea. During the Battle of Trafalgar she was engaged by Nelson himself, and she dueled with several British ships, surviving the battle. She later became part of the Spanish fleet, named Neptuno, after Spain declared war to France, and spent most of her life under the Spanish flag.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the French Bucentaure class ship of the line “Ville de Varsovie 1808”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Sails of Glory Ship Pack: Bucentaure 1803 / Robuste 1806
description:
Bucentaure was the lead ship of her class and was the flagship of Vice–Admiral Latouche Tréville, died on board on 18 August 1804. Vice–Admiral Villeneuve hoisted his flag on 6 November 1804 and on board he hosted the Franco–Spanish war council at Cadiz. At the Battle of Trafalgar she was commanded by Capt. Magendie and defeated by Admiral Nelson's HMS Victory, losing 197 men and with 85 wounded and after three hours of fighting, she surrendered to HMS Conqueror.

This ship pack can also be used to represent the French Bucentaure class ship of the line “Robuste 1806”.

Ship pack contains a new ship with base, ship card, ship log, and specific deck of maneuver cards.

—description from the publisher

Based on the latest scientific research from Positive Psychology, the Happiness Board Game teaches players how to raise their happiness levels. Players take turns rolling dice and moving around the game board. Game board is made up of three kinds of spaces: positive circumstances and attitudes, negative circumstances and attitudes, and bonuses.

When players land on a positive space they take a card from the corresponding deck and read it out loud. Cards present positive situations were points are rewarded for being in a positive circumstance or having a positive attitude. Those points are then multiplied by their corresponding weight (10% or 40% respectively) that the situation has on one’s happiness level. Players than move their score tracker the appropriate number of spaces in the scoring area.

The same happens when players land on a negative space, except the points are negative and suggestions are given on what players can do to raise their happiness levels in negative situations.

Some of the cards provide points of discussion such as, “You begin volunteering at the local animal shelter. Scientific studies have found that volunteering increases our level of happiness. Right now have every player name some volunteering work that he or she would like to do.”

Play ends with the first player to reach 100 points.

—description from the designer

Created by the Tallinn University Digital Safety Lab The idea of the game is to learn more about digital safety. The goal is to answer as many questions as possible and win after accumulating a required number of cards. The game is designed so the inquirer has to formulate a question and evaluate whether the answer is correct or not. Discussions and real life examples why this knowledge is important are encouraged.

At his/her turn, the player (inquirer) chooses and asks a question on the card from the next player (respondent). The inquirer evaluates whether the answer is correct or incorrect.
In case of CORRECT answer the respondent keeps the card and places it in front of him/her, face up.
In case of INCORRECT answer, the inquirer keeps the card and places it in front of him/her, face up.
Player who first collects 3 cards from one category (same color) shouts out "I'm Safe!" and wins the game!

—user summary

Hit me! is a comic book style push your luck card game. Players will be playing Hit! cards to make other players discard cards from their Life deck. The last player with cards remaining in their life deck is the winner.

Players begin with a small deck of face-down cards that make their life deck and a single Hit! deck in the middle of the table. During a player's turn, they draw a card from the Hit! deck and place it face up for all players to see. They continue to play cards until they choose to stop or they Bust and are out of the round.

If a player draws a Hit! card, they place it in front of any other player. If the current player stops before Busting, all other players will discard 1 card for each Hit! in front of them. All cards are added to a communal discard pile. If the Hit! deck runs out of cards. All of the cards in the discard pile are reshuffled to make a new Hit! deck. If a player draws a Trip! card, they place it in front of themselves. If a player ever draws 3 Trips! they Bust, and they instead discard 1 card from their life deck for each Hit! in front of all players.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Star Wars: Imperial Assault – Legends of the Alliance
description:
From the very first days of the Imperial Assault Core Set, players have been launched into the Star Wars galaxy, playing out the thrilling missions of the game’s narrative campaigns. These campaigns have continued through expansions like Twin Shadows, Return to Hoth, The Bespin Gambit, Jabba’s Realm, and Heart of the Empire. Though these campaigns took you to every corner of the Star Wars galaxy, there was still a common thread. In every campaign, up to four players took on the roles of Rebel heroes, battling a single Imperial player who commanded the forces of the Empire arrayed against you.

Now, with the Legends of the Alliance companion app, you have the chance to unite with all of your friends in a cooperative campaign. The Legends of the Alliance app takes over all of the functions once performed by the Imperial player, freeing you to experience entirely new campaigns in a fully cooperative environment with your friends as you fight side by side against the Galactic Empire!

description:
There Grew A Kingdom is a medieval theme, middleweight strategy board game for 2 to 5 players, and one game takes about 20 minutes per player.

It is a competitive 4xGame that uses mechanics such as area control, worker placement and resource management.

It has soft end game trigger, player powers, and card combat system.

The goal of the game is to get the most Glory Points.
Those you get for several actions that reflect the glory of your Kingdom.

The game area is made up of hexagons, which are placed randomly.

By activating resource areas, you get resources which you are using to build and upgrade the cities, as well as your army.

The army is an important aspect of the game.
It is used to protect your kingdom and expose other players.
Army battles are carried out using army cards.

This game does not use any dice!
Consequently, the game has a very low luck factor!
Your game is determined by your decisions and the players' reaction to them.
The only luck element is an Army Card Battle that you can try to avoid.

—description from the publisher

The preschooler version of the classic Pi0ctureka game with a giant mat

Pictureka! is a new kind of game, both in mechanic as in artwork. The mechanic is invigorating with its large square gaming tiles. The game board is composed of 3 by 3 game tiles, so the game board on which is played is a huge square of 63 cm x 63 cm. This is large enough for several players to be able to play the game at the same time. The game board is “alive”, it is not a static board as the tiles are flipped over, turned and moved. So it is not possible to memory map the board. The mechanic of an ever changing board makes it not only fun; also the challenge is kept into the game all the time.

A game turn 
Players will have to throw a color dice at the beginning of their turn. According to the color thrown (3 possibilities) they must draw a mission card of the same color. There are 3 kinds of missions.

How to win the game? 
Be the first one to have successfully accomplished 10 missions. Each successful mission gives 1 point, so be the first one to get 10 points. 
The artwork is something not to be found in ‘standard’ games. The personal drawing style of the artist Eugene makes this a kind of playing artwork.

The expansion adds an extra step to the game: in order to exhibit precious artefacts in their Museums, our Curators will now need to send their agents out into the world to bring them back first!

Players will have to choose which Continents to send their archaeologists to carefully, as funding expeditions doesn’t come cheap. However, if you don’t have any agents present, you can’t bring objects back from that Continent ! Unless of course, you can convince one of your competitor’s staff to lend a hand…

So how does it work ? The expansion’s board has spaces for each Continent, on which you can place your agent meeples to symbolize expeditions that you are sending out. Each player starts with one agent that they can place on any Continent they wish for free. Once you have an agent on site, you can bring objects back from that Continent as normal during the Exploration phase. If you have no agent on a particular Continent, but another player does, you can pay them 1 Prestige point to use their staff to do the work for you. If there are no agents present on that Continent, then you can’t bring anything back at all !

You can also pay extra to send additional archaeologists to a given Continent. If you do so, you gain additional bonuses, such as extra Prestige points when you draw a card. Each additional agent you send out to the same Continent costs more than the last. However, if you are the first to develop a full network of 3 agents, you gain a considerable bonus ! The second player to do so also gets a (smaller) bonus.

—description from the publisher

This entry is for the Kickstarter edition of Museum.  Packaged inside are several expansions that are not contained in the retail release:


     The Crystal Skulls expansion, which adds 12 Crystal Skull cards which can be shuffled into the artifacts and when collected can either be played as a regular artifact or can be sold to a special 'Crystal Skull Collector' for many points.
     The Assistants, each giving a different set-up and a unique ability.
     The League of Extraordinary Explorers, double sized cards that can be purchased for 8 prestige points and exposed in the Main Gallery, these cards also have a unique ability.
     The Solo mode.
     Discard Tiles, for placing cards in a player's storage.
     Extra Cards: 5 Expert cards, 3 Favour cards, 4 Headline cards, 12 Patron cards 


About the game:
It’s the turn of the 20th century, the golden age of museums. As interest in and accessibility of museums grew exponentially, many institutions underwent an intense period of expansion on both an intellectual and physical level, searching to grow their collections for profit and science.

You play as a curator of one such museum and it’s your job to build the biggest, most coherent collection that you can in this game of collection and bartering featuring over 300 individual illustrations by Vincent Dutrait and authentic architectural facts. But it’s no simple task!

Each player in Museum has a small collection of relics to get them started, after which, they will have to send explorers around the world to uncover others. These relics each have a value which is either the cost to add them to your museum, or how much they contribute towards adding other relics to your museum. “Spent” Relics are added to your reserve. You can withdraw them from it by exchanging them for an equal amount of items however your opponents also has access to your reserve!

During the game you will be required to assemble different collections. These can be from different categories (war, agriculture, architecture, etc) or periods (Ancient Egypt, Rome, Aztec, etc). Patron cards will give you bonus cards for amassing certain collections. Explorer cards will allow you to hire famous archeologists to confer bonuses to your museum and event cards will provide you with some game changing circumstances that you’ll have to work around, based on historical events!

All these different elements make compiling your collection an interesting and sometimes tricky experience! At the end of the game points are scored based on collections and their value and the player with the most points wins!

description:
User summary
パクッとプリッと (Pakutto Puritto) is a Japanese memory game where, in turn, you will have to find some animals' favourite food, while you take care of their poop.

An animal's beloved snack will become the topic, and you have to find it among the scattered face-down food cards. Turn up one card, and if that is its favourite food, you can take it and feed the animal. If it's not, sorry, put the card face down again.

As they have eaten, the animals will take a poop, and your place will become filled with poop cards. Find as much of the animals' favourite foods as you can and take care of the poop!

A game for children mainly, with bright attractive graphic design.

The second edition has only technical changes: a better rulebook, Furigana added the Kanji names on the cards, bigger cards and a bigger box, the game is now for 2-6 or more players, a game variant is included in the rules.

Our intrepid archaeologists have stumbled upon lost relics infused with the power of 6 Old Ones (one for each Domain in the game). These artefacts will now be corrupting your continent decks and have a chance to appear as cards are drawn. On top of their standard value and a domain, each object has a power that varies depending on which god it’s dedicated to. You can use these powers to bolster your museum's Domain Collections or hamper your rivals.

That’s not all however. Possessing these relics will cause you to earn “Occult” points. The player with the most Occult points at the end of game will score bonus victory points. On top of that, as soon as you gain 6 Occult points, you get to draw a “Disciple of the Occult” secret objective card (much like your Patron card) which will give you a chance to score even more points at the end of the game! Sounds good right? But beware, the Old One’s power comes at a price…

Playing the most powerful relics of a value of 5 will attract the eye of the terrifying beings that linger behind the veil of reality, with calamitous effects for your museum. These cards act as a sort of double-edged sword. Using Dagon's most powerful relic for example will flood your opponents' galleries, rending parts of them unusable. However, the Old One will return to take his due, and some of your gallery space will also disappear into the depths...

The expansion contains over 40 new components, including object, character, Notoriety and Event cards as well as some different effects tokens, all contained within its own expansion box.

—description from the publisher

Cults of Dagon is a print and play game of set collection and insanity for 2 to 4 players. The game is a work of fan fiction based on H. P. Lovecraft's story Dagon. The game is distributed for free, and anyone who is interested in playing it is allowed to make a copy for themselves and their friends.

In Cults of Dagon players control groups groups of 5 cultists traveling around the world to seek the knowledge and tools to summon the sea god Dagon. The tricky part is that too much knowledge may lead them to insanity, reducing their effectiveness for the rest of the game.

Each turn 6 cards are drawn from a deck of artifact cards. Each card represents a type of artifact (book, map, dagger, etc) and has a numerical value between 1 and 6. Players alternate placing their cultists on the cards. They cannot place cultists where they already have a presence, and must outbid by at least one the player with majority on an occupied card.

Once all cultists have been assigned, each card goes to the player with majority. The cards are arranged in sets (books with books, etc), and if the numerical value of a set reaches or exceeds the Insanity Value for that game, a cultist of that player goes insane and is removed from the game. The set that went bust is turned face down.

The game continues until the deck runs out of cards or a player loses 3 cultists. Players score the value of their face up cards. Each face down set is worth the Insanity Value of the game, regardless of card values.
Highest score wins the game.

This is a strategic level wargame on the 15th century English civil wars. The game system is identical to Taiheiki.

Players manage either the House of Lancaster or the House of York. 
Each leader counter represents historical kings, royalties and lords such Richard of York, Henry VI and Richard Neville. They affect battle, recruitment and area control.
During each turn, players move alternately their units based on initiative. After all movement is ended, battles are resolved, area control is determined and new armies are raised.

Victory is determined by victory points, gained mostly from area control.

Counters: 90 1.3cm/0.51 inch
Map: half standard map
Time: a year (15 turns)
Game area: all of Britain (excluding northern Scotland)

description:
Object of this game is to conquer as many constellations (groups of stars) as you can.  You conquer a constellation when you get your colored discs on more than have the number of stars in the constellation.

Play takes place on a 34" x 22" mat with images of the constellations.  Players roll dice to move their pieces around the track on the outside.  You either stop on spaces with color\star combinations that allow you to put a marker on a star or stars in a constellation or you land on spaces that allow you to take a card and follow the instructions on the card.

Two other variations of game play include The Game of Polaris (The North Star) in which players start in a different constellation and move towards Polaris.  Final variations include a Fast Game Variation which allows you to just conquer stars based on dice throw instead of moving around the track on the game mat.

—user summary

In the bleak, war-torn landscape of the not-too-distant future, a battle rages on. On one side, an army of the undead: former friends and family members who have succumbed to the zombie virus — decaying flesh and an insatiable appetite for destruction are all that remain. On the other side, a magic squad of unicorns: pixie dust and rainbow farts, their mythical horns as deadly as their enchanting glare. Which side will you fight for? Which will fight against you?

Unicorns & Zombies is a turn-based card game for 2-4 players which pits — you guessed it —unicorns against zombies. Players draw a trio of cards — weapons, gear, or action — and equip their fighter in the hopes of taking out the opposing teammate(s). Some cards can make your fighter nearly invincible. Others can make you want to flip the table over and rage-quit. You won’t though, because winning is more important than keeping friends. In groups of 3 or more, the fighter you pull is completely random, so your love of unicorns could be totally useless when you’re forced to fight as a zombie. And sometimes, you’re the only fighter of your kind on the table, so you’ll need to be strategic AF to win the round.

—description from the designer

This is an educational geography game for children that features an inflatable globe. There are three levels of play.

Game One is a simple picture matching activity with race element. Players attempt to match the picture on a card (like a Kangaroo) with a location on the globe (like Australia) as a timer is running. First player to the finish space wins.

Game Two is a race across the board as players attempt to match pictures and locations to a continent card (Asia, Africa, etc.) that was randomly assigned at the beginning of the session.

Game Three adds word cards and requires additional reading skills. This is also a race but it includes a card taking mechanic based on a word search between player's hands.

Designed for 2, 3, or 4 players age 3 or older.

Includes the inflatable globe, a globe stand, tokens, cards, timer, instruction booklet, and board.

boardgame: Pokémon TCG: Pokémon Card VS Expansion
description:
User Summary

Pokémon Card VS - ポケモンカード★VS is a Japanese-language expansion; it has no corresponding English-language expansion.

The expansion includes 151 cards.

The VS expansion was distributed in a non-conventional manner. While the cards were still packaged in foil packs, each of these packs contained 30 cards instead of the traditional 10 cards. This was also the first time the term 'half deck' was officially used and became a recognized method of gameplay. There were three random half decks released: Grass & Lightning - 草 雷ハーフデッキ, Water & Fire - 水炎ハーフデッキ, and Fighting & Psychic - 闘超ハーフデッキ. Each half deck contained a random assortment of set type Pokémon (i.e. one could only get a Fire-type Pokémon in the Water & Fire half deck). Colorless-type Pokémon and Trainer cards could be found in all half decks.

The 30 cards in each booster pack consisted of: 11 energy cards, 11 character cards, 6 trainer cards (2 of which were TMs), 1 holo character card, and 1 holo special energy card.

Wizards of the Coast officially stated that they had no plans to release the VS set, primarily because they had competitive play in mind. Some cards from this expansion were still released in English in the Tyranitar Half Deck; however, these cards were produced by MEDIAFACTORY and received a limited release to competitors of the 2001 Tropical Mega Battle. Despite this statement, Pokémon USA localized the Generation IV equivalent, Pokémon SP when they debuted in the Platinum expansion.

-- summary obtained from:  https://leonhartopedia.fandom.com/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_VS_(TCG)

Lost To Time is a real-time co-operative board game in which players must collect 4 keys to unlock the door leading to the unimaginable treasure. It is played in 10 real-time minutes with the possibility of gaining additional time as you play by rolling a set of dice and taking actions simultaneously. Players will use these actions to create a network of paths leading to all corners of the gameboard and travel on them to collect 4 Golden Keys. But, watch out! There are deadly traps along the way. If that wasn't enough, the ceiling is collapsing and giant blocks of stone are coming down to crush you.

Players will have to be quick and smart about making strategic decisions all while trying to survive! Run fast, help each other, and don't die!

Tens Solitaire is a quick solitaire game using a standard deck of 52 cards. Players remove pairs of card adding up to ten. Tens, Jacks, Queens, and Kings are removed in sets of four matching cards.

13 cards are dealt in two rows of five cards and one row of 3 cards. Remaining cards for a draw pile.

You may discard any pair of cards which add up to 10 or any set of four matching face cards or tens. Replace all discarded cards from the top of the draw pile.

You win the game if you can get all 52 cards on the table.

A quick combat squad game of 3 units each, where you see which mythological characters can best each other in battle (Egyptian and Greek currently, with more planned for the future).

No Dice. No Cards. No luck. Just pure strategy and choosing different team combinations.

Every turn, the player progresses each of their unit's turn meter (by moving the respective cube along the ready track) until they have a unit ready to attack. Each Hero has special abilities and attacks, creating an interesting strategy to every team. Each ability has a value (either damage, heal or alter turn meter) that the player activates and chooses to target.

Players win by defeating all of their opponents units.

Alternatively, more than 2 players can fight in a free for all, where each player is out for themselves.

Print and Play is available ahead of an upcoming crowdfunding campaign

Each player has 13 infantry companies, 2 light batteries and one heavy battery. At the beginning of the game, the pieces are set up on the red, blue and yellow marked spaces. Each turn the players move 2 of their pieces 1 space in any direction. The infantry has a firing range of 1 space, the light batteries of 2 space and the heavy batteries of 2 and 3 spaces. If a player has less than 4 pieces left, he has lost the game.

In Kaedama, you and a partner are new restaurant owners looking to spread the universal joy of ramen. You must manage customers, assemble bowls of ramen, and make enough money to someday buy the restaurant of your dreams.

In this 2 player co-operative game, one player is in charge of the 'Back of House' responsible for prepping and cooking food as well as washing dishes and plating. The other player controls the 'Front of House' by seating customers, upselling drinks & appetizers, and entertaining the guests. Each round, players will roll action dice to determine what actions they can take. However, in order to take an action, it must be paired with a limited pool of speed dice. You'll need to communicate and strategize on how best to use manage your time.

Be quick about it! No one likes a cold bowl of soup!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Dim Carcosa: Mythos Pack
description:
Dim Carcosa is the sixth and final Mythos Pack in The Path to Carcosa cycle for Arkham Horror: The Card Game.

Your investigation into the mysterious play The King in Yellow has led you across the globe and brought you to the edge of madness. Now, in the final act of The Path to Carcosa you travel beyond the cosmos to a world a place beyond all imagining. Before you lies a warped, alien city. Its twisting streets and aberrant architecture have no semblance of order or structure. In the distance, a murky lake reflects the glare of two suns. This is Carcosa–the realm of madness in the stars, where Hastur reigns supreme.

All shall be revealed when you travel to Dim Carcosa, meaning that the locations in this scenario have no unrevealed side. Instead, their reverse sides bear story cards to push your plotlines forward to their final curtain. In this lost realm, insanity is inevitable. But even with your mind fractured and broken, your investigation does not end here. In Dim Carcosa, you are not defeated when you have horror equal to or greater than your sanity. Instead, you journey on, facing impossible odds in the hopes to return home alive.

—description from the publisher

description:
Interpoly (2 player)
One player has 16 red pawns (policemen), the other player has 4 yellow pawns (gangsters). Movement is determined by a die. Red may share the die result between two or more pawns. The police tries to enclose the gangsters and the gangsters try to escape to the 4 home spaces in the corners of the game board. On the roll of a 6 red may roll again, yellow may take 1 red pawn off the board. 1 captured policeman counts 1 point, 1 enclosed gangster counts 2 points. After the game, the players switch their roles and after 2 games the player with the most points wins.

Kuddelmuddel (2 or more player)
4 red pawns are placed in the center of the game board, the rest are placed near the board. The destination spaces are marked with the letters A-F. Each player is assigned to one destination space. Movement is determined by a die. Each player may move any pawn. If he gets one on his destination space he keeps that pawn and places a new one in the center space.
The player who gets the most pawns to his destination space wins the game.

—user summary

Darkmace is a 2-4 players miniatures board game about dark ages' wars. You will control one of four armies aiming to conquer the most territory in the battle field. The types of combat units are infantry, archer, cavalry and catapult each having different moving, attacking and defending abilities. You must position them strategically across the battlefield to best coordinate your attacks and to enlarge your land.

Darkmace is played over 13 turns, with each turn consisting of move units, recruit units, construct buildings and manage gold steps. At the end of the last turn, the player that controls the most provinces is declared as the winner.

Darkmace combines miniature gaming and area control styles on one board. You must best position your units individually on hex spaces while taking the control of the provinces as a whole.

—description from the publisher

Last Blood is a turn based competitive or cooperative thriller where players explore, fight and run from monsters.  The game features exciting events and a very unique computer based art style.

The game is largely based on exploration and encounters with monsters.  A very simplistic combat and movement system allows fast play but with complex choices.

Players lose health from combat, starvation and poison.  The last player alive wins.

—description from the publisher

Eight Cards is a game where 2-4 explorers (players) go on an expedition for 7 days (turns) to try and earn the most reputation (points) and become the most legendary explorer (the winner).

Gameplay is simple to learn and play, but always changing and near impossible to predict. It works like this:

All players have 8 cards. These 8 cards are the same 8 cards as everybody else has in their hands. Each turn the players select a card and once everyone has selected their card, the cards are revealed at the same time and compared. Depending on the number of people who played the card, the outcome changes. Based on the number of players with the same card, positive outcomes can become negative, the target of the card may change and even special actions must be performed.

As the head of your expedition, your role is to figure out when others will play each card, bluff about when you're going to play your cards, and deduct when it's safest to play your cards based on what's been played already.

It's a game of reason, strategy and sometimes just plain luck to figure out how to build the best reputation and win the game.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Rabbit Trails: The Play on Words Game
description:
What is Rabbit Trails? Rabbit Trails is card game for all ages (especially students!) involving play with word association.

How do you play it? Play starts with each player being dealt 7 cards. Then the top card of the remaining deck is flipped over. Each player takes turns laying a card this is easily associated with the card showing. For example: The card flipped at the start of the game is, "Rabbit". Player 1 plays, "Carrot" (because rabbits eat carrots). Player 2 plays, "Chef" (because chefs can cook carrots). Player 3 plays, "Astronaut" (because both chef and astronaut are professions). And player 4 plays, "Grape". Wait! Grape?? Grape clearly doesn't match with "Astronaut". The other players hear out Player 4's reasoning and vote on whether or not to allow the card to stay in play. They vote for the card to be removed. Player 4 has to draw a new card as a penalty and play continues. The winner is the first person to get rid of all their cards. If a player cannot play (and does not want to try and fool the others by playing a less than perfect card), the player can draw and, if the card will work, play it right away.

But that's not all! There are also action cards featuring various ways to foil the plans of the other players and get you closer to winning the game.

What are the benefits of Rabbit Trails? Benefit #1, Rabbit Trails is an awesome game. Seriously! I have been designing this game over the past couple of years and have been getting bogged down with requests to make this game available from friends, family, and even people who see us playing it while out around town. After refining the cards and getting the rules nailed down, this game is finally ready for prime time! But that isn't all... Benefit #2, is related to education. It's not often that fun and education go together, but Rabbit Trails bridges that gap. Because the aim of the game is to get players to find connections between words, it makes students use higher order thinking, like analysis and evaluation to be successful, which are at the top of Bloom's taxonomy.Benefit #3 [Bonus], Rabbit Trails is great to test your English language skills if you are currently learning English or use English as a second language. It makes English language learners think critically about words and their multiple meanings!

—description from the publisher

description: The Dragon Lancers of the enlightened House of Shael Han are, once inducted into their elite ranks, are given ancient armor and spears that have been steeped in the essence of the great Celestial Dragons. Solitaire, print and play game. The Hero must inflict 8 wounds on the dragon before the dragon inflicts 4 wounds on the Hero.

The board shows the body of the dragon, divided in boxes with target numbers in them. The player places Aim cubes to indicate the area the Hero is targeting, and rolls dice. An aim cube must be removed to reroll the dice, narrowing the area that can be hit.

After rolling, the player uses the dice to attack the dragon and/or cast spells.

Next, the player rolls to determine the type of attack the dragon will perform, and resolves that attack.

The game continues until either the Hero or the dragon is dead. It *is* possible that they will both die in the same turn, ending the game in a tragic tie.

—description from the publisher

Early colonization efforts of Mars have been successful. With dozens of outposts scattered across the surface, a new challenge awaits: providing infrastructure to the red planet and moving goods to where they are needed most (while making a tidy profit of course). Fortunately the abundance of precious metals will help with this task by allowing upgrades to the transport vessels over time.

Tracks on Mars is a light-weight family crayon rail game. It has been designed specifically for two players. Drawing the tracks, loading goods onto the pawns, and over sized dice all work together to make this a very tactile experience. Luck is a major factor but can be mitigated by re-rolls and catch-up mechanics. On a strategic level, players have to balance network building, transportation, and equipment upgrades.

The core mechanics are Yahtzee-style dice rolling and resource allocation. Matching dice rolls are converted to currency which is then used to either claim new routes or move the player's pawns to pick up and deliver goods. Victory points are ultimately  scored depending on the distance that goods have been transported. Players have to decide whether they want to make multiple short runs for few VPs or spend turns building a long route for fewer high value transports.

This is a print & play title comprised of five dice, 40 resource cubes, currency token cards, an 18" x 18" board, and four 3D printed pawns.

—description from the designer

Unlike regular expansions, Generations booster packs could not be purchased individually from booster boxes. Instead, they were only available in special 20th Anniversary-related items of merchandise, which included Mythical Pokémon Collections and Red & Blue Collections. Each Mythical Pokémon Collection comes packaged with 2 Generations packs, in addition to a special Full Art promotional card and collector's pin featuring 1 of the 11 Mythical Pokémon available as part of the 20th Anniversary distributions during 2016.

The Japanese Starter Pack emulates the original both in terms of design and the random nature of the cards included. While the cards contained in each deck can vary, they are grouped based on evolutionary family and type. The deck can be broken down into two sections, which are packaged separately. The first section will always contain one of two Mega Pokémon, either M Venusaur-EX or M Charizard-EX, in addition to a fixed selection of Pokémon and Energy cards relative to its type, plus multiples of Trainer cards for a total of 27 cards. The second section contains 3 different groups of Pokémon containing an evolutionary family of 1 type, plus 1 other Pokémon of the same type, matching Energy cards, and a selection of single Trainer cards for a total of 33 cards. None of the cards available as part of the pack have rarity symbols. A special Blastoise version of the Starter Pack (Japanese: スターターパック カメックスver.) was later made available, initially exclusive to venues hosting the Kamex Mega Battle in Japan during May 2016, followed by a more general release from the Pokémon Center Online store in June 2016. This version substitutes the Grass or Fire-type cards available in the first section of the regular version with Water-type cards. These cards appear in the core Generations set, but are classed as secret cards in the Starter Pack. The Starter Pack also comes with an exclusive Chansey coin.

-- user summary obtained from: https://leonhartopedia.fandom.com/wiki/Generations_(TCG)

boardgame: Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey: Technology Starter Deck
description:
Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey is a new take on the collectible non-collectible KB line, with Jeffrey Neil Bellinger once again putting the rabbits through the grinder. Six new starter decks Animals, Crops, Elementals, Energy, Land and Technology will be available, and players can construct personalized decks of no less than 60 cards, with the cards from any deck.

Ultimate Odyssey is not collectible or random. Players can now buy starter decks and booster expansions for Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey and then use any combination of those cards to form their own constructed decks to play against opponents. Unlike in Killer Bunnies and the Quest for the Magic Carrot, where up to eight players play with the same deck of cards, all sharing the same draw pile, Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey has players each draw only from their own constructed deck of 60 cards. There are not any random packs, so players will always know which cards they are purchasing in a deck and they can buy the right products for their planned deck. For Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey six brand new starter decks (red, violet, blue, yellow, green, or orange) allow players to construct a personalized deck with any of the cards of any deck.

The setting for the new game is an ultimate race to build a bunny society on planet Earth. Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey pits players against each other as they struggle to gain Civilization Points, by building cities and increasing their bunny populations. Players gain points by developing their own civilizations or by using zany weapons and hilarious scenarios to destroy their opponents.

Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey pits players against each other as they struggle to gain Civilization Points, by building cities and increasing their bunny populations. Players gain points by developing their own civilizations or by using zany weapons and hilarious scenarios to destroy their opponents.

boardgame: Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey: Lively and Spry
description:
Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey is a new take on the collectible non-collectible KB line, with Jeffrey Neil Bellinger once again putting the rabbits through the grinder. Six new starter decks Animals, Crops, Elementals, Energy, Land and Technology will be available, and players can construct personalized decks of no less than 60 cards, with the cards from any deck.

Ultimate Odyssey is not collectible or random. Players can now buy starter decks and booster expansions for Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey and then use any combination of those cards to form their own constructed decks to play against opponents. Unlike in Killer Bunnies and the Quest for the Magic Carrot, where up to eight players play with the same deck of cards, all sharing the same draw pile, Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey has players each draw only from their own constructed deck of 60 cards. There are not any random packs, so players will always know which cards they are purchasing in a deck and they can buy the right products for their planned deck. For Killer Bunnies and the Ultimate Odyssey six brand new starter decks (red, violet, blue, yellow, green, or orange) allow players to construct a personalized deck with any of the cards of any deck.

Lively and Spry contains the Animals, Crops, and Land starter decks, as well as 3 limited edition promo cards.

When a tree is old enough, it starts to make flowers. The flower is a message from the tree to you, the pollinators. Calling you to come by day and by night – to drink its nectar, to pick up its pollen grains, and to transport these to another tree of the same species. Because it’s ready to breed.

Flight of Pollen is a semi-cooperative game where you pollinate as many trees as possible by visiting them with a variety of different New Zealand pollinators, with different pollinators being more successful under different weather conditions.  Rounds are split into day and night segments, with different pollinators active during the day versus the night.  Each pollinator must move from one plant to the next of the same species, moving pollen between them until all of the plants of the target species are pollinated- if any are missed, the game is lost.  One or more different native New Zealand plant are the focus for each of the four rounds, and as the rounds progress more pollinator species are added, as well as more plant species with more challenging pollination requirements.  If all of the required plants have been pollinated, then the team that collected the most berries wins the game.

Over each of the four levels different trees send out differing messages in their flowers. You’ll need to work together to complete each level, moving as the elements allow.

As flowers pollinated by you (or the wind) become seeds or berries, teams can collect the resulting berries.

Locally made, with recycled materials and FSC card

Welcome to the Trollz World! Join the Best Friends for Life as they scoot all around Trollzopolis collecting Gem tokens. But watch out for Snarf--Simon's half dog, half ogre sidekick--or he'll slow down your quest to defeat Simon!

To get around, players roll a die. Pimple Spell spaces and Snarf spaces can limit players' movement but can be gotten around with Obsidian tokens and Obsidian Spell Phone tokens, which can reroll dice or use the spell phone instead of rolling. Other spaces can call every player to a specific space. The Spell Phone included with the game decides what happens when it's landed on or rolled via a text message that appears on the screen.

The objective is to be the first player to collect all 5 Gem tokens and defeat Simon in the final showdown.

Based on the Trollz toyline and cartoon by DiC Entertainment.

A sequel to 5-Minute Dragon Slayer. This time, a 4-headed hydra is attacking your peaceful fantasy town!

Solitaire game. Each turn you choose 2 adjacent areas on the body of the monster to target, and roll dice. If you want to reroll you must remove one of your possible target areas, or lower your armor value by 1. Then you use your rolled dice to attack a part of the monster you were targeting, or to activate a spell.

Next, a die roll is used to determine which attack the hydra uses against you, and a second die roll to resolve that attack.

Continue until you kill the hydra, or the hydra kills your hero.

Downforce: Danger Circuit, an expansion for Downforce, consists of two new tracks on a double-sided game board as well as six new racer powers to shuffle with those in the base game.

Switchback Pass presents dangerous spaces. Cars may move through such spaces only to pass other cars and may not end their turn on one. Crosstown Speedway features two crossover loops, where cars in the rear can block cars ahead of them, and split areas of the track, where players must choose between the shorter single-lane section or the longer, more wide open section.

New powers are:

     Tough - On any player's turn, move 2 additional spaces when your car ends its movement next to a rumble strip (red-and-white).
     Bold - On your turn, if your car would move 1 or 2 spaces, move it 3 spaces instead.
     Defensive - If none of your cars move on your turn, move one of your cars 3 spaces.
     Reckless - On your turn, if your car moves diagonally between two adjacent cars, move it 2 additional spaces.
     Experienced - If any car starts its movement directly ahead of your car, move your car into the space they moved out of.
     Ambitious - On any player's turn, if your car crosses a betting line, move it 2 additional spaces.

description:
5 Stack is an exciting chip stacking game with the perfect mixture of strategy and chance. 5 Stack is simple enough that a 5-year-old can play it and interesting enough to captivate the seasoned board game veteran.  Proudly made in the USA, components include a game board, coloured wooden chips, dice, scoring pad, and instructions.  Designed by Jackson Robinson, and published by Kings Wild Project.

To set-up: Players all get 25 chips in their colour plus a single gold coin, and dice are rolled for a random set-up that adds five chips per player to a 6x6 game-board.

To play: In clockwise order players take turns to: (a) move a chip or a chip stack of their colour in a straight direction on the board; (b) place a new chip with the location determined by a dice roll. The game ends when someone has played all their chips and cleared all chip stacks under their control.

To win: The aim of the game is to score the highest number of points.  Points are scored by clearing a stack (when a stack under your control reaches 5 or more chips, with gold chips being worth 5 instead of 1 point each).  Your final score is the total number of "cleared" chips minus any of your unplayed and uncleared chips at game end.

Welcome brave fighters fighting for the freedom of the Rebellion! Another task is waiting for you. You have to decode the secret code which you received from the command and you must obtain secret information about Frist Order’s plans. 
In the game you will be playing  as Rey, Poe, Finn and Rose. Who will be the first to complete the mission? May the force be with you!

The goal of the game is to collect three white and three black information markers. At the moment when the first player puts the last of the six markers on his/hers  the character board, the game ends immediately and the player wins the game.

Each round of the game is divided into two parts: coding and gaining information. In the first part, players set up their code as quickly as possible to determine in what order players will take turns and how many actions they will have. In the second part, they do as much actions as they get after the first round: you can move your pawn, explore squares, rotate them, and gain information markers.

—description from the designer

This is a set of two new enchantments for Zauberei hoch drei. It was available at SPIEL'17 as a single card; the two tokens have to be cut out.

The new enchantments are:

     Tarnkappe ("Magic hat"): If you are on the stairs, your teammates can still help you this turn.
     Zauberkugel ("Magic Ball"): All Lumies revealed this turn will remain face-up until the end of the game.

Now play Crazy Eights crazier than ever!

Playing cards with numbers are history. In this new interpretation of Crazy Eights, emojis are the name of the game. Who will be the first to shed all their cards and win the game? Emojical Mau-Mau contains completely new and crazy action cards which put additional spice in the game. "Donating" and "Gambling" cards will drive your fellow players crazy – and get you on your way to victory.

Easy to learn rules - Quick game start - Great fun for the whole family!

—description from the publisher

Who will be the first and say some animal with "L"? Maybe the toothiest thing in the world with "S"? Or 3 things with "T" that is drawing in water?

Think it Up! Junior is famous party games for the little ones and contains task that will be understood by children.

—description from the publisher

In your efforts to exploit the riches of the Living Planet, you may find yourself in the midst of an industrial revolution. In this expansion you will find:
 20 x   pipelines
  4 x   Electricity Storage Units
  4 x   Pharmacological research units
  4 x   Oil Refineries
  4 x   Stell Foundries
  4 x   Weaponry Research Units 
  2 x   Industria Map Tiles 
  1 x   Rule booklet

At the beginning of the game, each opponent rolls the general/captain and places him in the captain’s area(Three circles). Then place the tie breaker die(clear with a small blue die inside it) in the tiebreaker area(5 circles) To start out each player rolls 2 of their dice and places them in the first row. Then the player can choose to either keep his/her row and try his/her luck or they can lower a die’s number by one and roll another die. The objective is to have a higher sum for each row than your opponent. The max capacity for each row is five die. At the end of the turn both players add up the die and the larger number is subtracted from the lower number. Next the Difference is taken away from the losing army. If a tie is to occur then the clear die with a blue die in it is rolled by each player and the sum of the two die is added to each player’s side based on the roll. Keep doing this until there is no tie. If you have a one in your row at the end of a turn change the one to a six before you add up. If you end up calculating before changing the one to a six the one stays a one for the remainder of the game. If you lose then you can roll the 20 sided die and re roll the captain. Place the same number of added die to your side of the military and try to beat your opponent.

Things to remember: 

     If you roll a one with your general, it stays a one
     Lower each die one at a time
     The game stops after you completely lose all of your die and roll the failure die


—description from the designer

Our explorers finally discovered that there is a high stakes to explore MYC.14.250’s seas. This expansion adds new possibilities, beyond lands... With a new vehicle and a new movement rule come more diversity and new strategies to master! You can now construct 3 new buildings (Oil rigs, Offshore Windturbines and Hydroelectric generators) and exploit rare Vibranium Mines hidden on some islands. Sail using the winds for free, or using an Oil and a movement action to go wherever you want (of course, within the movement limits). Oil becomes then an essential resource.

To perform an Explore action, you will either draw in the Land map tile pile or in the new Sea map tile pile. But beware, don’t expect a calm sea. A new cataclysm shows up you can’t protect yourself from: the Tsunami! Do not forget raging Tornadoes still there to knock you down… Careful! Discovering new horizons may be risky!

—description from the publisher

Arboria brings its own novelties, and a strong theme! This expansion is fully compatible with the two previous ones: Aquarius and Industria!

Arboria Expansion provides a new resource to exploit. Wood! But, as everything seems unusual on this planet, these trees are special… The sap they contain can be sold a fortune back on Earth. Burnt in a stove, it is twice more efficient than best wood you could use! And finally, travel time to bring it back to Earth allows its optimal drying! Here a sixth way to make rich!

Wood is a rare resource here. There will be less wood than the other resources. Cutting wood is pretty easy, sawmill produces wood on 2 die values. Watch out! Shortage and Market crash will occur more frequently…

Woods are thick and vehicles can’t move in… Furthermore, this wood is nearly impossible to extinguish when set on fire. A new cataclysm shows up: Forest Fires. Ferocious and brutal, they are only triggered in woods. Scientists didn’t find any solution to protect yourself from them.

Arboria also includes two special buldings: Botanical Research Centers and Shipyards to be used respectively in combination with Industria and Aquarius Expansions.

—description from the publisher

Baby is cooperative deduction micro game for two to three players in which one player facilitates the recurring and semi-random needs of the infant whilst the other one (or two) player(s) take on the role of the parents, attempting to decipher and resolve the cause of their child's cries.

The game consists of three micro decks of five cards each. One deck represents a happy baby, another a sad baby, and the middle a neutral baby. A baby status token moves between the decks, indicating the current climate of the infant and also defining from which deck the next card should be drawn. Cards are only seen by the player playing the role of the child, and upon them are depicted up to two different needs of the five in the game: hungry, poopie, sleepy, restless and crying wolf (ie. nothing's really wrong). Depending on the card the player will then place face-down tokens showing the baby is upset but not visibly indicating what the cause is. These needs can be resolved with different parent tokens, such as feeding, diapers, carrying, music and playing. While the need tokens don't come in multiples and therefore can't "double up" on the same time the parents only have a limited number of each of their tokens so they must use them wisely. After each step of the game (child expressing and then parents attempting to solve it) the baby status is always move to indicate the new state of the child. If a card should be drawn from an empty deck, the game has ended and the players all win if the status of the baby is happy.

This pack contains 5 cards exclusive to the Kickstarter campaign.

The cards are to be added to the base game.

The Proudest Unicorn
"When this card enters your Stable, each player must give you a card from their hand."

Eternal Youth Unicorn
"If this card is in your Stable at the beginning of your turn, replace a Unicorn in any player's stable with a Baby Unicorn. The replaced Unicorn goes to the Discard Pile.""

Legendary Flying Unicorn
"When this card enters your Stable, each player must DISCARD a card. If this card is sacrificed or destroyed, return it to your hand."

Unicorn of the Cosmos
"When this card enters your Stable, shuffle each player's hand and the discard pile into the deck. Each player must then DRAW five cards."

The Horniest Unicorn
"If this card is in your stable at the beginning of your turn, you may move a Unicorn from any player's stable to your Stable. At the end of your turn, move the Unicorn back to it's original stable."

"Steamroller" is a highly playable, tense, operational look at 1914's pivotal Tannenberg campaign. Command either the German 8th Army or the Russian 1st and 2nd Armies in the battle that decided the early course of the Great War on the Eastern Front. The game is the latest iteration of the system used in Hermann's earlier "Race to the Sea 1914", (as well as "Dead Reckoning") with corps, divisions, and brigades traipsing around the wilderlands of East Prussia and northeastern Poland.

Game components include one 11 x 17 game map, one sheet of 88 die-cut counters, and an array of cards used to bid for initiative and determine combat results.

boardgame: Best Allies: Rules for Gaming 18th Century Battles
description:
The 18th century in Europe saw a massive development in weapons and tactics. The musket was refined into a flintlock, smoothbore with a socket bayonet. Although accuracy was low, which limited effective fire to very short ranges, volume of fire improved considerably and units were capable of firing three and sometimes four rounds a minute. The infantryman was now capable of combining the roles of musketeer and pikeman in one. And to maximise firepower, formations became wider. The aim was to move a formation to within range of the enemy and then overwhelm them with firepower. Only rarely was a bayonet charge needed.

—description from the rulebook

Units of 3 bases represent an infantry battalion or cavalry squadron. Suggested basing sizes are given for 6, 15, and 25/28mm figures.

Players take turns activating one brigade at a time. Each brigade commander is rated as Poor, Average, or Excellent, which allows the commander to activate 1-3 groups of brigade units. After all brigades have activated, combat occurs.

The game includes army lists for various 18th century wars: Great Norther War, American Indian Wars, War of Spanish Succession, Wars Against the Turks, Jacobite Risings, Seven Years War, American War of Independence, and the French Revolutionary Wars.
family: History: War of the Spanish Succession

description:
Turbo Words is a fast paced word game for 2-4 players.

Players act simultaneously to fill individual playing boards with randomly selected lettered game tiles with the aim of creating as many words as possible.

Other game tiles force players to directly interact with each other adding to the intensity and enjoyment of the game.

The timer can also be started by any player... ensuring that the action is never allowed to slow down!

—description from the publisher

“When I grow up, I want to be…” This has been said, is said and will be said by children everywhere. There is a time when a child starts to discover the various professions and by the influence of a familiar or by the influence of some book or movie, she will identify herself with some kind of activity that she wants to do when she grows up.

Quando for grande QUERO SER… is a board game for 2 to 5 players, with ages of 5 and up. For this game 10 professions where chosen, those usually chosen by kids when completing the phrase. Those professions are: a Pilot, a Fireman, a Chef, a Police Officer, an Astronaut, an Engineer, a Mechanic, a Doctor, a Soldier and a Farmer. Some are represented by a female drawing and others by a male drawing.

The object of the game is to put together the various profession drawings that are composed of 3 parts: Head, torso and feet. The players need to have skill, attention and a bit of luck in order to gather on the board the three parts that are needed to complete each profession drawing. Besides the different drawings, the background color for each profession is also different in order to help the younger players to identify each of the parts for each of the professions so they can easily put together the complete set.
The winner will be the one who can complete the most profession drawings.

In Shoudo, two players face off across the battlefield of the Sengoku Jidai - the Japanese civil war. The players assume control of one of four great clans - Uesugi, Mori, Tokugawa, or Shimazu - and attempt to defeat their opponent to become the Shogun.

Shoudo is a terrain-based strategy game. The board, which consists of 40 cards, is shuffled and laid out in a random 5x8 configuration at the start of each game. Each player controls 3 armies which they may use to "burn" enemy lands and attack enemy armies and castles. The first player to destroy their opponent's Daimyo castle wins the game.

To win, players must engage in combat. When attacking an enemy army or castle, combat is fought over 3 rounds. Each round, both players roll a 6-sided combat die and add the strength of their unit and its terrain bonus - the higher roll wins the round, and the first player to win 2 rounds wins the combat.

Special clan abilities, inspired by the traditional clans, affect gameplay, giving each clan a unique advantage. These abilities can give tactical advantage or greater sustainability.

—description from the designer

New double-sided map for Mythic Battles: Pantheon. One side is the new Thermopylae Battlefield, while the other side features an even larger version of Poseidon's Sea from the Poseidon expansion. This side connects with the map from that expansion to form one continuous piece of art and an extra-large map for your sea battles.

boardgame: Shadows of Brimstone: Ancient Horrors Enemy Pack
description:
Tearing their way through the Void, these nightmare creatures are masses of writhing tentacles and ancient hate! Burrowing through realities, once manifested into a physical form, they lay dormant beneath the earth, sometimes for millennia; waiting with vile intent as they writhe in their hibernation. Most often, these Ancient Horrors are awakened from their dread repose by the smell of Dark Stone, or the call of some malign and eldritch being of great power, summoning evil to do its bidding.

This Enemy Pack contains everything you need to add these ancient and terrifying, creatures to your games of Shadows of Brimstone!  There are 2 Extra large Ancient Horror models included, as well as the large Enemy Record Sheet and all of the Threat Cards needed to add them to your games.

—description from the publisher

Empires have risen and fallen in the aftermath of the Great War, and Europa stands on the precipice of a new era. The economy is robust, morale is high, and defenses are strong. There are reports from the countryside of strange soldiers with glowing eyes, but they seem distant and harmless.

Scythe: The Rise of Fenris, the conclusion to the Scythe expansion trilogy, enables two different options for any player count (1-5 if you have Scythe; 1-7 players if you have Invaders from Afar):


     Campaign (8 games): The story of Scythe continues and concludes with an eight-episode campaign. While the campaign includes surprises, unlocks, and persistent elements, it is fully resettable and replayable.
     Modular (11 modules): Instead of—or after—the campaign, the new modules in The Rise of Fenris can be used in various combinations to cater to player preferences. These modules are compatible with all Scythe expansions, and they include a fully cooperative module.


While the exact nature of the episodes and modules will remain a mystery (most of these components are in 5 secret tuckboxes and on 6 punchboards), the components in this expansion include a detailed episodic guidebook, 13 plastic miniatures, 62 wooden tokens, 2 custom dice, 25 tiles, and 100+ cardboard tokens.

—description from the publisher

description:
All are playing with the purpose to take all the balloons to the sun before it is obscured by the cloud.
Place the sky tiles one after the other to form a path from the children to the sun. Place all the balloons on the starting tile. Place the cloud on the first sky tile.
The current player rolls the rainbow dice:
-Balloon face, the balloon with the corresponding colour moves forward to the following free sky tile;
-Wind face, you can choose which balloon to move;
-Cloud face, the cloud moves forward a tile.
If all the balloons arrive at the tile with the sun before the cloud, all players have won together!

Let’s put in order the highest number of toys exactly recalling where they have been placed.

Place the chest of drawers on the table. Place the 6 toy tiles facedown in front of the drawers. Take a set of 12 shoe tiles.
The current player takes a tile from the toy table, showing it to all, and puts it into any of the drawers. The turn passes to the next player, who will do likewise with the constraint of storing the card in the drawer below the previous one. The last two toy tiles stay on the table and will be considered as they were put on the floor.
Shuffle toy cards and assemble a card deck. Turn over all the cards, one at a time. All together the players should try to remember where to find that toy: place on the card a shoe tile with the colour of the drawer or with floor face up.

Now check the actual position of the toys: did you recall where all toys have been placed? You won!

boardgame: Mars Attacks: The Miniatures Game – Solo Play A.I. Deck
description:
The Martians have a mind of their own! Take command of the heroes of Greenville and team up against an AI-driven Martian force, powered by this deck of cards. Now you can play co-operatively against the Martians or face the threat solo.

—description from the publisher

This project was dropped and the game has never been published.

Day 1: It has been confirmed that what is now called "gravity stones" arose last night. Crystalline rock formations levitate in mid-air, resulting in a great decrease in the gravity around them.The potential benefits are enormous and the seven days we have to study them won't be wasted, be sure of it.

Gravity Stones is a development and optimization game with multiple choices in a brief playing time. The game plays over the course of seven rounds. Each round, a simultaneous first phase allows the players to play cards from their hand on the action side or on the resource side, also discarding one of them onto the central (company)

The second phase is individual, and starts with the first player, who uses their actions and resources (if needed) to establish a camp on the mountain, to study the stones, or to construct ZeroG tools from them. Once your actions are spent, you can either recover one of the cards left by the players or leave them; you will be punished or rewarded based on your choice.

With its intertwined actions and grant system, Gravity Stones allows you to augment your base actions with the stones you get back from the central board. The company that's hired you will also give you objectives to fulfill...or not.

Game 1: Prärie-Kampf (2 players or 2 teams)
Each player or team starts with 8 pawns on the red and blue outher spaces of their camp area. The goal is to get 4 pawns into the 4 camp area spaces in the corners of the enemy camp. Movement is determined by rolling a die. If a pawn gets on a space with an enemy pawn, the later gets captured. If a pawn reaches a destination space, one friendly captured pawn gets free.

Game 2: Verfolgungsjagd (Pachisi, 2-4 players)

Sirens split the black... BATTLESTATIONS! Control mighty battlecruisers facing off against each other in deep space. Use programmed actions to launch fighters and torpedos, fire lasers, and even unleash your railgun! Every move counts when the cold embrace of space awaits outside...

Players control two battlecruisers fighting across an imaginary grid of 3x2 cards. Weapons are played into the columns and move over to the opponent's cruiser in an attempt to cause damage. Each player has a command queue that they load cards into the back of and execute from the front of. This queue is played face up so your opponent has an idea of what you intend to do, but each card has two options so you are never sure what is going to happen.

It works like a two-player space invaders, with weapon cards moving across the space between cruisers and causing damage if they reach the other side. With only three hit points for each cruiser, it is intended to be a quick dueling game.

Mystic Vale: Conclave provides the ideal storage solution designed to hold all of the previous releases, with room to spare for future expansions! The new Equinox mechanic allows players to greatly speed up gameplay and can even scale up to 6–player games with the two additional starter decks provided. The new conclaves grant players the option to customize the starting decks of advancements and vales, creating a new play experience each game. Unique totem cards imbue players with awesome abilities they can use each turn, to truly feel the power of Gaia’s blessing. Spirit counters provide a way for players to track their spirit symbols during their turn.
